content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

February 6, 2002

Denese Matthes, Director
Boulder Creek Recreation
& Park District
P.O. Box 249
Boulder Creek, CA 95006

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-02-010

Dear Ms. Matthes:

This letter is in response to your January 14, 2002, request for written advice. Because your request is limited by its language to voting procedures embodied in Public Resource Code Sections 5782.16 and 5782.17, we regret that we are unable to provide you with answers to the questions you pose.

The Fair Political Practices Commission exercises jurisdiction conferred by the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). ¹ Our advice is available solely for matters arising under the Act,² which generally include conflicts of interest, lobbying, campaign reporting and other related subjects. However, the questions you raise read as follows:

"Boulder Creek Recreation & Park District has a five (5) member board. A quorum is three (3).

"If a quorum is present and the vote is two (2) YES and one (1) NO, does the motion pass?

"If the vote is two (2) YES and one (1) abstention, does the motion pass?"

Since the Commission's jurisdiction is limited to that conferred under the Act as amended, we are unable to respond substantively to your request. You may wish to

¹ Government Code §§ 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, §§ 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
² Subsection (b)(8)(D) of section 18329 of the Commission's Regulations (copy enclosed).

File No. I-02-010
Page No. 2

consult with the state Attorney General's Office regarding the proper application of the
Public Resource Code to the questions you raise.

If you have any questions regarding matters raised in this letter, please be free to
contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Kenneth L. Glick
Staff Counsel, Legal Division


KLG:jg
I:\AdviceLtrs\02010

Enclosure

sections.question: Boulder Creek Recreation & Park District has a five (5) member board. A quorum is three (3). If a quorum is present and the vote is two (2) YES and one (1) NO, does the motion pass? If the vote is two (2) YES and one (1) abstention, does the motion pass?
sections.conclusion: Since the Commission's jurisdiction is limited to that conferred under the Act, as amended, we are unable to respond substantively to your request. You may wish to consult with the state Attorney General's Office regarding the proper application of the Public Resource Code to the questions you raise.
sections.question_synthetic: The requester asks whether a motion passes in a five-member board with a three-member quorum when the vote is 2 YES and 1 NO, or alternatively 2 YES and 1 abstention.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC declines to provide advice because the questions concern voting procedures under the Public Resource Code, which falls outside the Commission's jurisdiction limited to the Political Reform Act matters such as conflicts of interest, lobbying, and campaign reporting.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Declination letter citing lack of FPPC jurisdiction. Question and conclusion sections clearly identified in document text. Document dated February 6, 2002, File No. I-02-010.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Boulder Creek Recreation & Park District has a five (5) member board. A quorum is three (3). If a quorum is present and the vote is two (2) YES and one (1) NO, does the motion pass? If the vote is two (2) YES and one (1) abstention, does the motion pass?

CONCLUSION: Since the Commission's jurisdiction is limited to that conferred under the Act, as amended, we are unable to respond substantively to your request. You may wish to consult with the state Attorney General's Office regarding the proper application of the Public Resource Code to the questions you raise.

embedding.summary: The FPPC declines to provide informal advice to Boulder Creek Recreation & Park District regarding voting procedures and quorum requirements, as these matters fall outside the Commission's jurisdiction under the Political Reform Act.
